data.title: Fifth Third: Q2 Report Shows Net Interest Income Growth Sacrifices Deposits
data.description:
Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB) announced Q2 earnings Thursday morning with revenue of $2.25B and an EPS of $0.90, beating expectations slightly. The stock rose slightly pre-market, but opened slightly lower and fell in morning trading. The bank raised its net interest income outlook, looking to a 6% annual rise, with lower anticipated charge-offs around 45 bps. In general, Fifth Third did not report a large improvement in guidance, but a narrower range of expectations around projections.

Its efficiency ratio fell to 56% from 61%. Although a minor issue, this is largely overshadowed by the rise in its net-interest margin to 3.1% and a higher return on assets. It also had slight lending expansion, but a very small decrease in deposits. FITB's reported tangible book value per share for Q2 was $20.89, up almost $1 from Q1, or about half of its current share price.

I covered Fifth Third last in January in "Fifth Third: Watch For Commercial Credit Risk Metrics In Upcoming Report." I had a bearish outlook on the stock due to concerns regarding commercial and industrial loan delinquencies, leaving the stock potentially overvalued given its high price-to-book value ratio. Since then, its charge-off ratio has stabilized around 45 bps, but its nonperforming asset ratio is concerning at 72 bps, up from 55 bps in Q2 24, down from 81 bps in Q1.

FITB is up by about 3.9% since the time I covered it. It fell sharply during the spring correction to ~$33, indicating significant cyclical risk exposure, but recovered fully since. Looking forward, I think investors and analysts should continue to keep an eye on macroeconomic trends relating to nonperforming loan risks. Further, Fifth Third's deposit trend is a concern, particularly because its profit expansion is largely driven by lower deposit costs, at the expense of a notable decline in deposits.

Deposit Competition May Challenge NIM

The key bullish signal in FITB's Q2 report was the improvement in net interest income, with guidance suggesting continued profit growth in lending. Its net interest income rose slightly, while its net interest expenses declined marginally. The average yield on its interest-earning assets rose to 5.18% from $5.13%, while the rate paid on interest-bearing liabilities was down to 2.78% from 2.8%.

Commercial and industrial lending was nearly unchanged, while its commercial mortgage loan portfolio grew 9% YoY and its commercial leases, only $3B in assets, were up 21%. Mortgages are up 4% YoY, while home equity and indirect consumer secured loans are up 12%. Total consumer lending rose 7% since Q2 24. Overall, we're seeing more growth in alternative lending categories that account for a smaller portion of its balance sheet, with plateaued demand in the largest and most liquid lending markets (mortgages and C&I).

The more concerning trend is the decline in its deposits to $163.5B, from $167.2B in Q2 24. Deposit losses were staggering -54% YoY in CDs over $250K. Though those only account for $2.2B in deposits, the decline signals that people with larger savings levels are fleeing Fifth Third, likely due to low rates. According to SmartAsset, Fifth Third's savings account offering only pays a 1 bps interest rate, far below the national average, with many smaller banks paying close to the 4% SOFR rate today. The bank's interest checking and savings deposit levels were both down 2% from Q1, with declines in all segments besides money market and demand deposits.

Its Q2 data showed interest-bearing checking liabilities had a 2.7% cost, whiles its savings deposit rate was oddly lower at 48 bps. Money market rates were 2.4%, while CDs were closer to 3.5-4%. Although it is paying interest on deposits, even if the "preferred checking" account APY is 1 bps (for its HQ zip code), indicating the bank is not looking to expand through offering attractive borrowing rates.

For now, we're not seeing a massive exodus of depositors. However, Fifth Third's deposit profile is concerning, with the vast majority of its deposits coming from demand deposits and interest checking, and only around 16% from CDs and savings. In the short term, this mix is good for Fifth Third's lending margin, allowing it to borrow from depositors at 2.4%, or about 2% cheaper than the US Treasury borrowing rate.

However, I argue this is unsustainable as a rise in depositor liquidity needs due to a change in economic conditions may lead to a rapid decline in deposits, with minimal savings and CDs to provide for a stable capital supply. Further, unless interest rates decline significantly soon, we should see Fifth Third's deposits continue to decline as clients move money to higher-paying accounts. As you can see below, Fifth Third's deposit trend defies that of banks in general:

The above data does not account for the continued deposit decline in Q2. In my view, Fifth Third is betting on more substantial interest rate cuts, preemptively shifting its depositor mix toward low-cost sources. If interest rates are headed back below 2%, then Fifth Third's depositors will be ideal, offering low costs today while not having a high risk of leaving if bank APYs are headed lower. However, if the Fed does not cut rates and APYs rise, FITB's deposit rate will be uncompetitive and will need to either rise or create liquidity funding risks.

To some degree, this mirrors the trend in US savings accounts in general. Compared to most higher-rate periods, the banking market since 2022 has been extremely abnormal as savings account rates rose marginally compared to prime lending rates. Savings account rates rose 3-5% for many smaller banks, but the largest retail deposit banks successfully maintained deposits at ~0% APY, perhaps because people are so conditioned to not earning interest on deposits.

At any rate, Fifth Third's deposit APY, though low, is not far from the national average, which has decline due to rate cuts. The market is currently looking at a total rate reduction of 41 bps over the next two years based on the 2-year to 1-month Treasury spread. See below:

In my opinion, many banks may not be right in assuming rates are headed back to pre-2022 levels. The bond market is only pricing in one or two rate cuts. Rate cut odds were even lower until recently, as Trump has increased his effort to replace Powell with a more dovish Fed chair who may cut rates as significantly as the President seeks.

With that in mind, there is reason to anticipate larger rate cuts, but a 2%+ cut may also cause the US dollar to decline at a faster pace and thereby stoke inflation. Thus, I argue that the rate cuts needed to stop deposit losses for FITB may also stoke other economic risks. Further, as long as the Fed remains independent, it does not appear the market expects such cuts, meaning FITB's deposits may continue to decline.

From Fifth Third's Q2 investor call transcript, we can see its loan-to-core-deposit ratio was up to 76%. That is not a high ratio based on banks' historically usual levels, but it is higher than that of most large banks today, reflecting FITB's greater focus on lending than securities, and its weaker deposits.

Credit Risk Not Worsening, Not Improving

Shifting to credit risk, Q2 showed small improvements, but not enough to clearly break the upward trend from 2024. See below:

FITB had $853M in NPLs last quarter, but its net charge-offs remained low at $139M. In general, Fifth Third's approach is low-risk, low-reward, focusing on loans that are not more diverse and not leveraged in companies and people with good credit. I expect that, if credit risks rise, they will rise more slowly or later for FITB than for many other banks.

Nationally, bank delinquency levels were rising from 2023-2024, but are showing some signs of improvement:

Between FITB's credit performance data and the national trend, I think it's fair to say that the increase in credit risk in 2024 has been arrested. To a degree, that aligns with the stagnated trend in unemployment. My core bearish argument against FITB in January was the expectation that this issue would increase in 2025, yet that is not what we're seeing. Thus, my outlook on FITB is upgraded to neutral, given I do not expect it will lose value unless loan loss expectations rise.

That said, although we're not seeing credit risk metrics rise, I'm not convinced the long-term risk is low. The post-COVID economy has seen notably higher consumer borrowing and lower savings. Falling rates and inflation have eased this problem, but this remains subject to unemployment, which may be poised to rise given lower labor productivity levels.

In my opinion, the credit market may be in a temporary uptick coming out of lower inflationary pressures on consumers and businesses. Assuming credit risk lags consumer sentiment, last year's higher sentiment level and the early 2025 uptick in the manufacturing PMI may have temporarily boosted lending market stability. Since we've seen the PMI fall back into contraction territory with lower consumer sentiment, however, most notably, CapEx expectations have utterly collapsed, now below mid-2020 lows:

In the short term, such as the next six months, we're not seeing robust signals of higher delinquencies on both consumer and business lending segments. Although this boosts FITB's 2025 earnings expectations and stability, most leading economic indicators remain somewhat bearish, with the CapEx trend being the most concerning. Large CapEx reductions not only lower economic growth and associated job demand, but also reduce lending demand and signal that some companies may have overspent on capital investments.

To be clear, FITB is not a high-risk bank based on its balance sheet. However, its leverage is elevated, with a Tangible Common Equity ratio of ~5.5%. The odds of a huge increase in loan losses are low, but even a small increase could jeopardize its tangible book value. See below:

FITB's valuation remains very high, 2.2X its tangible book, which is around 50% higher than its typical valuation range. Its yield is typical at 3.44%, with a forward "P/E" of 12X. Its high income largely offsets its higher price-to-book valuation, but that is subject to its ability to maintain a high net-interest margin, which I doubt due to deposit risks.

The Bottom Line

Overall, my view on FITB is upgraded to neutral because, despite my expectations, its lending risk profile has not risen, while the national trend in loan loss risks has improved. That said, I would still avoid FITB because I do not expect this will continue long-term. The economic data, though not terrible, is far from ideal, potentially on consumer sentiment and capital investment expectations.

Fifth Third's valuation appears largely dependent on its high and growing net interest income; however, that requires a higher degree of leverage and very low deposit costs. In my view, its NIM will fall by 2026 as it may need to raise deposit rates to maintain deposits; large rate cuts would improve deposit stability, but likely harm its NIM through lower interest income.

data.title: Why Opendoor's Huge Surge Needs A Reality Check (And Why It Might Last)
data.description:
Opendoor Technologies Overview

Last month, I upgraded Opendoor Technologies Inc.’s (NASDAQ:OPEN) stock from Sell to Hold, citing the “strategic shift from capital-intensive iBuying model to asset-light agent partnerships.”

There were some positive signals from Opendoor’s first quarter 2025 earnings as well, including improved operational efficiency and reduced losses.

Despite these glimmers of hope, there are still major hurdles ahead for the company, like achieving profitability, a “bogged down” real estate market, and the looming possibility of a NASDAQ delisting.

OPEN has since soared by 200%.

The following article reveals that OPEN’s recent stock performance, while primarily driven by speculative “meme stock” dynamics, may be a sign of more to come should its strategic shifts bear fruit.

Recent Developments

Since OPEN’s tripling, Opendoor has not had much news to report outside of a date (August 5, 2025) for its second quarter 2025 earnings report. As we have witnessed in the past with stocks like GameStop (GME), the combination of coordinated (social media) retail interest and a high short interest is a good recipe for a “short squeeze.”

Turning to the U.S. housing market, as reported by Redfin (RDFN), there were some encouraging trends in June. The median sale price rose just 1.1% year-over-year to $447,435. On average, homes can appreciate around 3-5% each year. In light of relatively high interest rates, a slower pace of appreciation keeps homes affordable. Compared to June 2024, the number of homes sold in June 2025 was up 3.3%, implying a subtle easing of the “lock-in effect,” which I described in my last article as a homeowner’s unwillingness to sell due to the prospect of higher interest rates. On that front, the national average 30-year fixed mortgage rate fell 0.1 point year-over-year, sitting just below 7% (6.8%). As for U.S. housing supply, the number of homes for sale is up 12% year-over-year, sitting above 2 million for the first time since the pre-pandemic period.

According to the National Association of Realtors, additional relief is expected in 2026. Its Chief Economist, Lawrence Yun, predicts that

“new-home sales will rise by 10% in 2025 and by 5% in 2026, the median home price will climb by 3% in 2025 and by 4% in 2026, and that mortgage rates will average 6.4% in the second half of 2025 and 6.1% in 2026.”

Meanwhile, Opendoor released its "Key Agent" app on July 15. Opendoor states that the app will

“help agents present cash offers to clients with increased speed and precision by providing an intuitive, streamlined home assessment process.”

So, this plays into Opendoor’s strategic shift to a hybrid agent-assisted selling model, which is designed to be less capital-intensive than pure iBuying. It is also more realtor-friendly than its direct-to-consumer iBuyer model.

As I discussed in my previous analysis, the strategic shift is likely a necessity to stay in business. The pure iBuying route has proven to be an unprofitable venture (for Opendoor, this has resulted in an accumulated deficit of $3.8 billion), with larger players, like Zillow (Z) and Redfin, abandoning the iBuying ship. Opendoor’s primary competition is now Offerpad (OPAD), which is also pursuing a more hybrid model. Interestingly, Offerpad’s stock is also in on this relief rally. Notably, Opendoor has been more resilient than Offerpad. Opendoor’s first quarter 2025 revenue of $1.153 billion represented a 2% year-over-year decline. Meanwhile, Offerpad’s $160.7 million in revenue in Q1 represented a 44% year-over-year dip.

Recall that Opendoor’s second quarter of 2025 outlook called for Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $10 million to $20 million, which Opendoor hasn’t achieved in three years. So, Adjusted EBITDA in Q2 will be a critical indicator of the company’s progress.

OPEN Stock Financial Health

Financially, Opendoor remains challenged. According to Seeking Alpha, its Altman Z-Score of 0.74 implies that Opendoor is “likely (...) headed for bankruptcy.” The score is based on a few financial ratios and predicts insolvency risk for the next two years.

Opendoor’s financial health continues to deteriorate amid consistent losses. As of March 31, its cash and equivalents were $559 million, decreased from $671 million at the end of 2024.

Its “current portion” of long-term debt was $946 million. Long-term debt was $1.565 billion. Opendoor’s debt is tied to its homes (non-recourse asset-backed). So, this is a bit different than conventional debt that the Altman Z-Score is usually basing its prediction on. If Opendoor can’t repay, lenders can only claim specific homes used as collateral, not other assets. On that note, Opendoor’s inventory balance increased to $2.4 billion as of March 31, 2025.

The company is actively focused on cost efficiency, which has helped reduce its operating expenses. As I calculated in my last article, Opendoor’s trailing twelve-month cash burn was $58 million per month, implying a cash runway extending into just early 2026 (based on historical data).

However, Opendoor may be able to secure funding by adding additional non-recourse, asset-backed debt. Alternatively, as of its last 10-Q, Opendoor’s at-the-market, or ATM, equity offering program allows it to sell up to $200 million in common stock. Given the stock’s recent resurgence, it would not surprise me to see the company dip into its ATM.

Conclusion

To wrap up, Opendoor’s meme-fueled rally is also partly backed by subtle macroenvironment relief and a strategic shift. It would not surprise me to see the rally continue for some time, especially should the company achieve its goals when it reports its second-quarter earnings.

On the other hand, as the rally is not tied to any major fundamental developments, it would also not surprise me to see Opendoor’s stock fall back below $1.00.

To be honest, it’s pretty unpredictable, as implied by OPEN's laughably high stock beta (24M) of 2.36.

From my porch, OPEN remains a “binary bet” and is only worth holding in a barbell portfolio until the company has clear visibility towards a sustainably profitable path.

data.title: El Pollo Loco Holdings: Traffic Issues Justify A Downgrade
data.description:
As a self-described foodie, I must say that I am disappointed in myself. I like to keep up on my investment prospects, even those that I don't end up investing in. And yet, it has been approximately two years since I last wrote about El Pollo Loco Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:LOCO), an operator of limited service restaurants that traces its roots back to 1980. In my last article about the company, published in late July of 2023, I viewed the company in a favorable light. This was in spite of the fact that comparable store sales leading up to that point had been mixed, and was driven by both valuation and the prospect of additional growth.

Since then, my decision to reaffirm the company as a Buy candidate has fallen short of expectations. The stock is actually down 2.7% since then. That's far worse than the 26.2% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) experienced. The fact of the matter is that, while growth for the company continues, bottom line results have been mixed recently. Management is targeting additional expansion this year. And in the long run, the restaurant probably will do fine. Shares are cheap, but I am a bit concerned about declining foot traffic at its locations. In light of this, I believe that assigning it a Hold rating as opposed to a Buy rating is appropriate at this time.

Time for a downgrade

The management team at El Pollo Loco describes the company as a limited service restaurant that focuses on fire grilling citrus marinated chicken. It serves up this food in the form of Mexican-inspired entrees like burritos and tostadas, salads, and more. Management describes the limited service restaurant industry as one that works to offer the food and dining experiences of fast casual restaurants, but with the speed, value, and convenience of quick service restaurants.

Over the last couple of years now, this concept has continued to achieve some growth. From 2023 to 2024, for instance, revenue expanded from $468.7 million to $473 million. The company benefited from a rise in company operated restaurants from 172 locations to 173, and from a rise in franchised locations from 323 locations to 325 locations. In total, this took the system wide location count for the business up from 495 to 498. Historically speaking, these restaurants range in size between 2,200 and 3,000 square feet, are capable of fitting between 50 and 70 people in them, and typically have a drive-thru.

The rise in location count was not the only thing helping the top line for the company. While the firm clearly benefited from this expansion, as well as the franchise advertising fee revenue and franchise revenue that both increased in response to the larger number of franchised locations, comparable store sales growth was also a factor. From 2023 to 2024, system wide comparable store sales grew by 3.2%. That followed a 5.9% increase the previous year. Franchised locations reported a 3.5% rise. And company operated locations enjoyed a rise of 2.8%.

Within the company operated figure, however, we see some interesting results. During 2024, the average check size at the firm's locations jumped by 7.9%. This followed a 2.3% rise the previous year. But the number of transactions contracted by 4.7%. This followed a 2% drop the year before that. Honestly, I really don't like seeing traffic decline like this. I know that inflationary pressures and stretched consumer budgets have not been positive for certain types of spending.

In fact, just recently, I wrote an article about Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) and its McLane Company subsidiary that operates as a food and non-food distributor. In that article, I detailed how declining restaurant sales had negatively affected the business, with those tied back to broader economic concerns. The fact of the matter is that it is cheaper to eat at home. So when times are tough, you should expect a decline in food-away-from-home spending.

With revenue rising, profitability for the company increased. But that increase was marginal, with net profits inching up from $25.6 million to $25.7 million. Things would have been better had it not been for the fact that interest expense jumped from $4.8 million to $5.9 million on a year over year basis while general and administrative costs grew from 9% of revenue to 9.8%. Franchise expenses also increased, rising from 8.2% to 8.9% year over year. Other profitability metrics for the company improved also. Adjusted net profits expanded from $24.5 million to $26.8 million. Operating cash flow expanded from $40.7 million to $46.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $41.7 million to $43 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business grew from $57.4 million to $62.7 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, the only data that we have covers the first quarter. During that time, revenue came in at $119.2 million. That represents an increase of 2.6% compared to the $116.2 million reported the previous year. The company benefited during this time from a rise in company owned locations from 172 to 174, and an increase in the number of franchised locations from 323 to 325. However, system wide comparable store sales contracted by 0.6%. This was driven by a 1.3% drop in franchised comparable store sales. However, even company owned locations saw an increase of just 0.6%.

As you might expect, this involved weakness from a transaction perspective, with the number of comparable transactions dropping by 3.8%. It was only because of a 4.6% increase in average check size that company owned locations avoided a decline like franchised locations experienced.

Even though revenue for the company increased, profitability pulled back, with net income contracting from $5.9 million to $5.5 million. Franchise costs grew from $10.6 million to $12.4 million, more than offsetting reductions elsewhere. Other profitability metrics ended up weakening as well. Adjusted net profits, for instance, dropped from $6.8 million to $5.5 million. Operating cash flow was slashed from $11.2 million to $4.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $10.7 million to $9.1 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company declined from $15.7 million to $13.9 million.

For this year in its entirety, we don't really have much to go with. We do know that management expects the number of system wide restaurant locations to rise by between 10 and 11. Between 9 and 10 of these will be franchised. The other location will be company owned. Management does expect to spend between $30 million and $34 million on capital initiatives. This will include the remodeling of between 60 and 70 restaurants throughout the year. Outside of this, we don't really know what to expect.

Management did say in their latest quarterly release that menu pricing is expected to rise by about 3% year over year. That means a slight weakening in the second half of this year to a year over year increase of just 2%. The firm is also looking at new menu innovations in order to boost traffic. But given how traffic has been over the last couple of years now, I am not terribly optimistic that this will bear fruit.

If we project out financial results based on performance for the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow of about $36.6 million and EBITDA of $55.5 million. Using these estimates, and also using the historical results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. On an absolute basis, these multiples are certainly not bad. But relative to other similar enterprises, the picture is a bit more confusing.

In the table below, I compared El Pollo Loco to five similar enterprises. On a price to operating cash flow basis, three of the five companies I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper than it is. But on an EV to EBITDA basis, our candidate was the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
El Pollo Loco Holdings 9.0 7.2
Potbelly Corporation (PBPB) 14.0 15.1
Dine Brands Global (DIN) 3.8 7.3
Jack In The Box (JACK) 6.6 20.2
Denny's Corp (DENN) 6.1 7.9
Shake Shack (SHAK) 34.2 44.9

Takeaway

Considering how shares of El Pollo Loco are currently priced, and considering the continued growth that the company is achieving, you might be surprised that I am downgrading the stock. Certainly, shares are priced at levels that I would consider to be attractive. But honestly, I am worried about the reduction in foot traffic. Continuously hiking prices while experiencing a corresponding decline in foot traffic can only go so far. And until we see evidence that the picture has stabilized there, I believe that downgrading El Pollo Loco shares to a Hold makes the most sense.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: The Worst Is Baked In
data.description:
Company Background

Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) is the second largest pharmaceutical company in the world by market capitalization at $377B. The company has two operating segments: Innovative Medicine and Medical Devices. Innovative Medicine is engaged in the development, production, marketing and distribution of compound therapies in Immunology, Neuroscience, Infectious Diseases, Cardiovascular/Metabolism, Oncology, Pulminary Hypertension, and Global Public Health. Medical Devices develops, manufactures, markets and distributes devices in the areas of Cardiovascular, Orthopedics, Surgery and Vision. Total annual sales are about $89B with Net Income of $14B in 2024. Annual FCF (Free Cash Flow) is about $20B.

The company spun off its Consumer Health business in 2023. The stock was only down about 9% from its 52-week high before Q2 results were reported due primarily to the overhang of legal claims.

Nonetheless, the Q2 results showed the company’s stability by posting modest gains on both the GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) top and bottom lines.

A snapshot of reported earnings compared to consensus estimates, including the increased full-year guidance:

Both the Innovative Medicine and Med Tech segments posted sales gains in the quarter.

The diversity of J&J’s offerings is a major strength that is hard to match and provides greater stability than companies that focus on only one or two areas.

The Elephant in the Room

The obvious overhang created by the uncertainty of J&J’s legal battle over claims of harm from asbestos in its talcum powder products is creating a drag both in terms of brand perception and potential payouts. J&J attempted to settle the outstanding ~63,700 active cases (and a total of ~90,000 claims) with a payment of $8.9B, but was not accepted.

The company attempted to avoid the claims by spinning off the division that controls the products against which the claims are against, but the court did not allow J&J to offload the responsibility and continues to hold the parent company accountable.

It is impossible to estimate the actual future compensation that J&J will have to pay to affected claimants, cut it is important to understand that whatever the outcome is, it is likely to be no more than a temporary setback to an entity the size of J&J.

The stock will probably take a hit if the settlement is in excess of $15B, but I suspect the final outcome will be less than one year of Free Cash Flow for the company (~$20B). My reasoning is that I believe it to be unlikely that the final settlement will be much more than double the original offer from J&J. If I am wrong, the stock could sustain more substantial (but temporary) damage.

If I am correct, then J&J will need to borrow a good portion of that sum to make the payment and the added interest will become a minor drag on EPS (earnings per share) going forward. And, assuming that interest rates drop in the future, J&J will be able to refinance that debt at lower rates to reduce the cost over the remaining term of the debt.

In other words, while this is a significant event in the near term, it is unlikely to make much of a dent in J&J’s long-term future results. It could, however, provide a good buying opportunity for those who are patient enough to wait for the outcome.

Growth Prospects & Pipeline

The pipeline is significant, as it has to be in order to maintain the sales of a colossus like J&J, but I want to start with the immediate future based upon achievements in the most recent year.

R&D (Research and Development) is what drives future sales and this is an area where J&J excels. 27 regulatory approvals for medicines and 15 major medical device launches in one year. Most pharmaceutical companies would be satisfied with a small fraction of such achievements, but this is what happens in a company that has 26 products or platforms that generate over $1B each per year.

Two more interesting facts: ~25% of sales are derived from products launched in the past 5 years and ~75% of sales come from products that are either #1 or #2 in global market share positions. The current consensus estimate for future long-term (3-5 years) EPS growth is about 8.75%, but the overall growth rating is an F. That is because of the expected payout for legal claims over the coming 12-18 months which is expect to turn the EPS growth negative for a year, without creating an annual loss.

I am not as optimistic about the EPS growth as the consensus and probably because I know that consensus estimates always come down over time. So, my expected EPS CAGR over the next five years is 6% from current levels. That will take into account the temporary set back from legal issues and assume the growth trend gets back to normal after that.

I won’t list everything in J&J’s pipeline because it is simply too long, but I will break out a few highlights. In 2025, the company expects 15 potential drugs approvals in the EU & US. The Innovative Medicine management plans 8 submissions to the US and EU for drug therapies, has ten compounds in Phase III trials and ten more in Phase I or II trials. Many of the compounds are listed more than once since some are either approved, being submitted or in trials for multiple applications for different diseases. But each approval either opens up another new stream of revenue, expands or extends an existing one.

The slide below provides a glimpse into what management sees as its primary drivers of growth from Innovative Medicines over the next few years.

The MedTech segment expects beneficial growth from the Shockwave acquisition and advancements across its cardiovascular devices. Management believes it can continue to grow revenues and control costs with the end goal of consistently providing greater value to its shareholders. J&J has generated ~$95B in FCF over the last five years and has returned over 60% of that FCF to shareholders in the form of dividends and share repurchases.

Speaking of which, let’s take a look at the dividend history and future prospects.

The Dividend

J&J has raised its dividend for 63 consecutive years, the latest increase coming on April 15 th of this year in the form of a 4.8% increase. Over the last five years, J&J has raised its dividend by an average of 5.4% and by 5.8% over the last ten years.

The payout ratio remains very manageable at 49% and the balance sheet is strong with the company maintaining a credit rating of AAA from Standard and Poors.

My expectation is for the company to continue to raise the dividend at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of ~5% for at least the next five years. With the current dividend yield at 3.35%, the yield on investment five years from now would be around 4.28%; in ten years it would be ~5.5%.

Since I am retired and 75 years of age (and counting), I like rising dividends that increase at a rate faster than the rate of inflation and J&J fits that bill for me.

Valuing J&J Shares

Now I will value the stock using two different methods. First, I want to use the DDM (dividend discount model) using the 5% dividend growth rate. My DDM formula looks like this:

(D/(M-X)) = V

Where:

V = Fair Value

D = Current Dividend

M = Discount rate (I use my required rate of return on investment)

X = Expected future average annual increase in dividend

(5.20/(.09-.06)=173.33

I expect mature company stocks like J&J to provide a total return of about 9%, the current dividend is $5.20 and the expected growth in the dividend is 5%. When we plug these numbers into the model the result is $173.33 per share, slightly above the current share price of about $165. This indicates that the price is undervalued, selling at a discount to fair value of 4.8%. I believe that the price will achieve its fair value within the next five years so my expected annual compound average gain will be 1.8% for the valuation increase. The Excel formula is:

=((173.33/165)^(1/5))-1

If we add the 1.8% annual valuation gain to the dividend yield of 3.35% and the expected EPS growth rate of 6%, we end up with an expected compound annual average growth rate of 11.15%. We could probably do better but not much without accepting greater risk.

Now I want to look at the value from a multiple perspective, assuming that the PE ratio will revert to its historical mean over the next five years. The 10-year average PE for J&J is 17. The current PE is 14.6, so the stock is trading at only 85.9% of fair value. From this assessment we find that fair value is $180.71. So, if we calculate the compound average annual increase from a multiple expansion, we get 3.1%. The calculation in Excel is as follows:

=((17/14.6)^(1/5))-1

Then we add to the annual expected valuation increase of 3.1%, the current dividend yield of 3.35% and the expected EPS growth rate of 6% to find the expected compound annual total return of 12.45%. That result is better than the DDM estimate, but with the relatively low risk, I’d be happy with either one since I am not focused on just appreciation, but total return, and especially in adding another layer of rising income to my retirement savings.

The layering of future income that grows faster than the long-term rate of inflation is one of the primary areas of focus in my book, “Making Wall Street Irrelevant – Successful Investing Made Simple,” due to be released by early August.

Risks

There are two primary risks that have kept investors away from J&J for several years and will continue to be factors for a few more.

First, the legal issues that surround the talc/asbestos claims have created a lot of uncertainty over the past few years. According to management, 95% of the claims have been settled. According to the latest on this front, management seems to be too optimistic. J&J attempted to reduce future legal exposure to future claims by creating a spin off that contains the products in question called Kenvue (KVUE). There is, however, the possibility that new claims could be filed and that the courts will not accept the transfer of responsibility from the parent to the offspring (so to speak). But I suspect that the worst will be behind the company by mid-2026, and as time passes (probably 1-3 years after the final settlement), this cloud will dissipate like the morning fog on a sunny day. That’s not to say that the reputation hasn’t take a big hit (it has), but that the brand, tarnished as it may be now, should be reparable over time.

Second, the sales of J&J’s leukemia drub Imbruvica peaked in 2021 just under $7B and have fallen substantially each year since to $709 million. J&J recently received approval of an Imbruvica extension in frontline mantle cell lymphoma, but this won’t improve the future prospects of the drug by much since such cases are rare. Investors saw what was a blockbuster drug (in terms of sales) lose market share to competitors and bolted. There is always the possibility of this sort of thing happening again with any pharmaceutical company. But I believe that J&J has a pipeline that can overcome such occurrences and continue to grow.

Conclusion

J&J is appropriate for long-term investors seeking rising income with a starting yield of 3.35% from a high-quality company. I already own a position in J&J, but will not add more until there is greater clarity on the legal claims outcome. Until then, if I did not own J&J, I would consider starting now with a half position with the intent to add more when a settlement is announced. However, I think much, if not all, of the downside is already baked into the price at current levels. As the saying goes, “Sell the rumor, Buy the news.”

I rate J&J a Buy.

data.title: CuriosityStream: Are Recent Developments Cause For Concern?
data.description:
CuriosityStream (NASDAQ:CURI) shares are up by more than 100% since I last wrote about the video streaming company this year, when an AI catalyst emerged for CURI stock.

Yet while 2025 has been yet another banner year for this comeback story among SPAC stocks, shares were at even higher prices as recently as June, and there have been some developments that could be viewed as cause for concern.

Still, despite the possibility for there to be some substance to the market's more cautious stance on CuriosityStream, other factors suggest that the bull run may not necessarily be over.

Regarding one factor that may have already raised red flags among investors, subsequent news may prove sufficient to assuage such concerns. When it comes to another valid concern regarding this stock, recent remarks from management could assuage these concerns as well. That said, while these factors may not be as worrisome as they seem on the surface, it's possible that CURI's current valuation, which continues to factor in future potential, limits possible short-to-medium term upside relative to current prices.

CURI Stock: Recap and Recent News

At the start of this year, CuriosityStream was primarily a turnaround play, with the "global factual content" purveyor shifting to a monetization model that would more readily get the company to a state of profitability.

However, only a few weeks into January, CURI stock suddenly went from turnaround play to AI play, when the company announced its first deal to license its content for AI training purposes. Still, while at the time I saw this development as something that could shift sentiment back to highly bullish, that didn't exactly happen.

Although CURI did rally in the weeks following this news, the stock bounced between $2 and $3.50 per share from February through April. While not for certain, macro factors weighing on the market during this time may have played a role in this price performance.

That said, even as the market didn't immediately go highly bullish again for CURI on the AI news, such a sentiment shift arrived on May 6, following the release of CuriosityStream's Q1 2025 results. For the March quarter, the company reported a 26% YoY jump in revenue, largely due to a 523% increase in "Library Sales," the category the company uses to classify the licensing of content for AI model training purposes.

This big jump in "library sales" helped to counter a slight decline in revenue for the company's main streaming business, and more importantly, enabled CURI to hit an important milestone. For the first time, CuriosityStream reported positive net quarterly earnings.

While said earnings ($300,000, or 1 cent per share), weren't that far above breakeven, this could mark the start of a permanent move to steady profitability, especially as the company's content amortization costs go down.

The fact that CURI once again increased its quarterly dividend further indicates that management is confident that earnings and operating cash flow will continue to improve.

With this in mind, it's no shock that investors reacted positively to the earnings release. In the weeks following Q1 2025 earnings, CURI stock zoomed from around $3.30 per share, to as much as $7.15 per share.

Investor Sentiment Once Again Takes a Breather

After the earnings release in early May, CuriosityStream finished out the month with yet another positive development. On May 27, the company announced that its shares were being added to the Russell 2000 and Russell 3000 indices.

But while investors reacted positively to this news development, not too long after this, CURI stock began to give back its latest round of gains. Since hitting a new 52-week high on June 3, CURI has fallen by around 37.9%.

This comes despite further positive announcements from the company, including a new series of international licensing agreements with third-party broadcasters and streaming companies, as well as the declaration of a 10 cent per share special dividend.

Admittedly, there is likely more at play here behind this pullback besides profit-taking. Investors could be turning bearish on CuriosityStream, for two reasons. First, since early June, key insiders like founder/Chairman John Hendricks, as well as Stinchcomb, have disclosed plans to sell some of their personal holdings in CURI stock. This insider selling has likely been viewed by the market as a sign that shares have potentially topped out in price.

Alongside concerns related to insider selling, concerns about the runway for CuriosityStream's AI licensing strategy may be rising again as well. It doesn't help that, with shares up significantly over the past year, CURI is not exactly in the "undervalued" category anymore.

CuriosityStream Stock Valuation

In my last CURI stock write-up, I analyzed CURI's then-valuation. In a nutshell, valuation at around $2 per share was very reasonable, relative to both the company's trailing twelve month EBITDA at the time, as well as relative to potential improvements in profitability from the AI licensing catalyst.

For the March quarter, CuriosityStream's EBITDA came in at around $3.6 million. With CuriosityStream guiding for Q2 adjusted free cash flow of between $2 million and $3 million, at or above reported levels during Q1 2025, it may be reasonable to anticipate EBITDA coming in at similar or perhaps even higher levels during Q2.

If CURI's AI revenue stream proves steady and the company continues to prioritize cost cutting and maximizing the bottom line, it may even be reasonable to anticipate the company reporting similarly strong EBITDA numbers for the latter half of 2025.

In other words, it may not be far-fetched to believe that CuriosityStream's annual EBITDA will come in at around $14.4 million for the full-year ending Dec. 31, 2025. Yet while this would represent a big improvement compared to 2024, one can argue that, even after the pullback, such growth is well-baked into CURI's valuation.

Currently, CURI has a market cap of around $249.3 million. Add $4.2 million in capital lease liabilities and subtract CURI's $33.4 million cash position, and total enterprise value comes to around $220 million. With this, it's very much possible that CuriosityStream is currently trading at a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of around 15.7x.

Most other entertainment stocks are trading at EBITDA multiples in the high single-digits. For CURI to sustain its valuation, the company will likely need to hit the top end of revenue and cash flow guidance during Q2, then report a further growth resurgence during the back half of the year.

Otherwise, shares will likely languish at current prices, or worse, give back further gains, if subsequent results fail to impress. That said, based on certain factors and developments hinted at earlier, I am less concerned about the situation taking an unexpected turn for the worse.

Some Silver Linings from Press Releases and the Post-Earnings Conference Call

Put simply, I believe any concerns about insider selling with CURI stock are overblown. Even after disclosing a planned sale of several million dollars worth of shares, John Hendricks continues to own a 37.3% stake in the company.

Clint Stinchcomb owns 4.3% of outstanding shares, with his personal position worth over $11 million, substantially above the $1.1 million or so worth of shares he disclosed plans to sell in the aforementioned Form 144 filing. Not only that, CuriosityStream just extended Stinchcomb's employment contract for another four years.

For agreeing to stay in the CEO role through at least 2029, Stinchcomb is receiving 2.4 million performance-based RSUs, providing him with a substantial incentive to level up on CuriosityStream's turnaround and AI-based success.

Speaking of which, concerns about CuriosityStream's improving results stalling out, or worse, deteriorating once again, may be overblown as well. At least, based on some statements made by Stinchcomb on the Q1 2025 earnings conference call. Responding to an analyst question about the AI licensing pipeline, the CEO noted that CuriosityStream remains in talks with other potential licensees, suggesting that an even greater level of AI-related revenue may lie ahead.

Responding to another AI-related analyst question, Stinchcomb noted that the company has yet to utilize AI to reduce expenses such as translation costs. This specific statement suggests that CuriosityStream may have even more room to cut costs and maximize profitability.

Final Thoughts and the Bottom Line

The market may be having second thoughts about CuriosityStream shares right now, but if the silver linings mentioned above do indeed pave the way for more positive surprises ahead, CURI could overcome an already-rich valuation and perhaps make its way back to its 52-week high.

Such an event could arrive as early as next month, when CuriosityStream is scheduled to next report quarterly results. For a move to even higher prices, CuriosityStream will really need to knock it out of the park, in terms of both AI-growth and improved performance for the company's legacy business.

Although patience may be necessary, consider CURI stock still worth holding if you already have a position. For those who have yet to buy, if shares remain rangebound or even experience a further pullback now and ahead of earnings, you may want to consider initiating a position in this name.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: 9.6% Investment Grade Dividend Yield From Private Credit
data.description:
Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (NYSE:BXSL) is a highly defensive BDC with a near-double-digit dividend yield and a healthy credit performance profile. The BDC last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.77 per share, kept unchanged from the prior distribution and $3.08 per share annualized for a 9.6% dividend yield. I last covered the private credit ticker in the winter of 2023, with total returns since then positive. BXSL remains at the top of my income buy list against a future resumption of Fed rate cuts that will pressure BDCs. The CME FedWatch Tool expects rates to have been reduced by 50 basis points by the end of the year, with the floating rate loan exposure that typically underpins private credit loans set to see yields dip in response to this. To be clear, a possible greater pace of rate cuts demands more selectivity on portfolio allocation to BDCs. The need for this will be muted if tariff-driven inflation does not materialize greatly to drive Fed caution on cuts.

BXSL had a 98.8% allocation to floating rate debt as of the end of its fiscal 2025 first quarter. Further, 98.2% of its investments were first lien senior secured debt with a loan-to-value ratio of 47.4%. The BDC is backed by Blackstone (BX), the world's largest alternative asset manager with $1 trillion in assets under management. Crucially, this provides BXSL with significant scale in loan origination and sourcing. The near-100% focus on first lien loans also means credit risk can be better managed in the event of debtor default as these loans are secured against the debtor's assets. BXSL generated first quarter total investment income of $358 million, up 17.8% over its year-ago comp but still a small miss of around $400,000 on consensus estimates. Net investment income ("NII") per share at $0.83 was a beat on consensus by 6 cents.

Dividend Coverage, NAV Premium, Credit Quality, And Risk

NII at $0.83 per share means BXSL is 108% covering its dividend, a roughly 93% payout ratio. Bears would be right to flag that coverage has been dipping, falling by roughly 100 basis points from 109% coverage in the fourth quarter of its fiscal 2024. The risk here is that pending cuts to the Fed funds rate would force coverage to slip even lower, with BXSL having kept the base dividend the same since late 2023. BXSL was asked about slipping coverage against the specter of rate cuts during their first quarter earnings call. Management replied that they will focus on reducing general and administrative costs and their cost of funds to buffer NII. The BDC does not intend to broadly tap its spillover to maintain the dividend and stated it would adjust this quarterly payment downward in the event.

BXSL reported NAV of $6.2 billion, around $27.39 per share, flat sequentially with a 17 cents per share hit from net realized and unrealized loss countered by an 11 cents per share gain from accretive stock sales. BXSL currently trades hands for $32.43 per share, a roughly 18.4% premium to its NAV per share as of the end of the first quarter. BDCs that trade at a premium to NAV occupy an enviable position in the space as they're able to diversify the source of funds by selling their common shares at a level where it's accretive to NAV. BXSL was rated investment grade at "BBB" by Fitch Ratings as recently as April with a large $12.8 billion investment portfolio at fair value as of the end of the first quarter.

The BDC's weighted average yield on performing debt investments at fair value was 10.2%, a 20 basis points dip versus 10.4% in the prior quarter. BXSL had just 0.1% of its investments on non-accrual status at fair value as of the end of the first quarter, far below its peer group average of 1.2%. This forms a critical pillar for my long position in the commons, as cuts to the Fed funds rate in aggregate with possibly rising credit stress would more than likely force more intense dividend cuts for some tickers in the BDC space. In terms of being on the defence with income plays, BXSL ranks highly. The BDC has an extremely high quality underwriting record, which in aggregate with its large debt investment portfolio and fully covered dividend yield provides reasons to remain long. The coverage is tight, and I'd be watching upcoming earnings to see whether this is expanded or further shrinks. This presents the most salient risk for current shareholders as it would mean sustained pressure on NII. BXSL is still being rated as a buy.

data.title: Cintas Continues To Chug Along, Shares Consolidating
data.description:
We have long been a lone bull on shares of Cintas Corporation (NASDAQ:CTAS), most recently getting bullish again on the stock following its plunge in December 2024. We had called for a buy at $185 in December, and got behind the stock again a few months ago just over $200. A lot has happened this year, including walking away from its takeover of UniFirst, while focusing on expanding the core business. While that takeover would have added synergies, the price tag was eye-popping. Shares traded up another 11% or so from the last buy call, but have been consolidating in recent weeks. In this column we check back in on the performance of our house position in Cintas following the just reported fiscal Q4 earnings.

The chart suggests shares want to test $200 again, which is where we think there and below new money can re-enter. Not much has changed in terms of how this trades. Fundamentally for many on basic metrics it is "too expensive," but the growth is quite positive despite the premium valuation. Yet, there really isn't a reason why it cannot stay at a premium valuation. Many stocks do for many years. And Cintas is a leader in its niche. Let us touch on performance.

Cintas delivers a top- and bottom-line beat against expectations once again

Once again, the company delivered a double-line beat versus consensus estimates. The revenue line was a small beat of $40 million, while the EPS beat was a decent $0.02 higher per share versus expectations. Revenue was $2.67 billion, rising 8.1% compared to $2.47 billion a year ago. The organic revenue growth rate for fiscal Q4, which adjusts for the impacts of acquisitions and foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, was a stellar 9.6%. Revenue was also up sequentially as well from fiscal Q3.

Expanding margins once again

We know that gross margins can't expand in perpetuity, however the recent trend has been extremely positive, and in fiscal Q4 we saw expansion once again. Really stellar trend here folks. Gross margin hit $1.33 billion compared to $1.22 billion a year ago, an increase of 9.1%. This was up from $1.32 billion in the sequential quarter as well, which is extremely positive. Year-over-year comps are strong and seeing sequential growth is positive. We also like that the gross margin as a percentage of revenue was 49.7%, rising 50 basis points from 49.2% a year ago. Ideally margins get and stay over 50%.

Income expansion

Cintas is still in growth mode. Operating income in fiscal Q4 was up 9.1% to $597.5 million vs. $547.6 million a year ago. Operating income however dipped from the sequential quarter, which may be why shares are reacting a bit poorly today. The operating income margin as a percentage of sales was 22.4% in Q4 2025, compared to 22.2% a year ago. This was also a sequential decrease from 23.4%. This is perhaps another reason shares were lower. But from last year we saw earnings growth. Net income was $448.3 million, up 8.2% from last year. Overall EPS hit $1.09, surpassing estimates by $0.02, compared to $1.00 a year ago. So, growth remains, but has slowed some at least in terms of the pace of growth. Still very positive though.

Dividends and repurchases

Cintas also pays a growing dividend, and is buying back shares. The company just paid a total of $611 million in dividends this fiscal year, an increase of 15.2% from the amount paid last fiscal year. While the yield is low, we love long-term dividend growth. And the company bought back $679.3 million worth of stock in the fiscal year.

Looking ahead

As we look ahead to fiscal 2026 we expect ongoing growth. The company sees revenue expanding to $11.0 to $11.15 billion, up from the $10.34 billion it brought in for fiscal 2025. We saw $4.40 in EPS this fiscal year, and for fiscal 2026 the company thinks it can achieve $4.71 to $4.85 in EPS. This assumes no acquisitions in the fiscal year, no new repurchases, and no substantial swings in the economy. From our perspective the company stock trades like a few others that are slow and steady growers, but enjoy premium valuation. There is a rich premium here so you are paying up for this growth. But the stock has long enjoyed such a premium. We think you let shares come down under $200 and do some fresh buying in $10 blocks. This is an investing approach we teach at BAD BEAT Investing. New money is best be served waiting for a little more retracement, and in this macro environment that's certainly possible.
